corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: This collection consists of writings, notebooks, calendars, scrapbooks, research materials, correspondence, professional papers, teaching papers, personal memorabilia, juvenilia, family papers, photographs, printed materials, audiovisual materials, computer media, and other papers documenting the creative work and life of writer, artist, and performer Wayne Koestenbaum. Writings include drafts, proofs, and other papers documenting Koestenbaum's twenty books of fiction, poetry, criticism, and opera libretto. Writings and research materials include extensive materials relating to Maria Callas, Harpo Marx, Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, and Andy Warhol. Correspondence includes letters from many literary figures, including extensive incoming and outgoing correspondence with Jeanne Schinto. The papers include documents, letters, and pieces of memorabilia documenting the history and lives of members of the Koestenbaum family. Printed materials include numerous contributions, interviews, reviews, and other appearances of Koestenbaum and his work.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Wayne Koestenbaum on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2018, 2019.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 4, 88, 101 (computer media) Restricted fragile material. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Boxes 19, 87, 100, 113, 116 (audiovisual materials) Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Wayne Koestenbaum Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Wayne Koestenbaum Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into seven series: I. Writings, 1960-2018. II. Notebooks, calendars, and scrapbooks, 1980-2010. III. Research materials, 1940-1999. IV. Correspondence, 1976-2017. V. Professional papers, 1960-2017. VI. Personal and family papers, 1889-2015. VII. Printed materials, 1971-2018.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Wayne Koestenbaum and assistants performed extensive processing and description work on his archive before transferring it to the Beinecke Library. Under the guidance of Beinecke Library archivists, Mr. Koestenbaum reviewed, arranged, and described his papers in detail at the folder or item level, housing them in archival supplies provided by the library.

After receipt, Beinecke Library staff reviewed the housing of the collection and made adjustments as necessary to meet preservation and collection management requirements. An archivist made a rough sort of the boxes into broader series, but made few changes to the folder-level arrangement of the archive, except where necessary for proper housing or to separate audiovisual and computer media per the library's preservation best practices.

Beinecke Library staff wrote the collection- and series-level description. Almost all description at the file and item level were written by Wayne Koestenbaum or his assistants, with minimal edits made by library staff.
This finding aid may be updated periodically to account for new acquisitions to the collection or revisions in arrangement and description.

bioghist.head: Wayne Koestenbaum
bioghist.p: Wayne Koestenbaum (born 1958) is an American poet, critic, and novelist as well as a painter and musician. His work spans a wide gamut of topics and genres, ranging from poetry, cultural criticism, and fiction to opera. Examples include Jackie Under My Skin: Interpreting An Icon (Farrar, Straus, and Giroux, 1995), The Milk of Inquiry (Persea, 1999), Andy Warhol (Lipper/Viking, 2001), Model Homes (BOA Editions, 2004), Hotel Theory (Soft Skull Press, 2007), and The Anatomy of Harpo Marx (University of California Press, 2012). The Queen's Throat (Da Capo Press, 2001) was a National Book Critics Circle Award finalist. Koestenbaum wrote the libretto for Michael Daugherty's opera Jackie O, which premiered at the Houston Grand Opera in 1997. He received a B.A. from Harvard, an M.A. from Johns Hopkins, and a Ph.D. from Princeton; he won a Whiting Writers Award, and was a co-winner of the Discovery/The Nation Poetry Prize. Formerly an Associate Professor of English at Yale and a Visiting Professor in the Yale School of Art's painting department, Koestenbaum is a Distinguished Professor of English, French, and Comparative Literature at the CUNY Graduate Center in New York City.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into twenty-two subseries: Double Talk: The Erotics of Male Literary Collaboration (Routledge, 1989), 1978-1989; Ode to Anna Moffo and Other Poems (Persea, 1990), 1985-1990; The Queen's Throat: Opera, Homosexuality, and the Mystery of Desire (Poseidon, 1993), 1989-1993; Rhapsodies of A Repeat Offender (Persea, 1994), 1990-1993; Jackie Under My Skin: Interpreting An Icon (Farrar, Straus, and Giroux, 1995), 1993-1995; Jackie O (1997), 1997–2006; The Milk of Inquiry (Persea, 1999), 1992-1999; Cleavage: Essays on Sex, Stars, and Aesthetics (Ballantine Books, 2000), 1991-1999; Andy Warhol (Lipper/Viking, 2001), 1998-2001; Moira Orfei in Aigues-Mortes (Soft Skull Press, 2004), 2000-2004; Model Homes (BOA Editions, 2004), 2000-2004; Best-Selling Jewish Porn Films (Turtle Point Press, 2006), 1997-2005; Hotel Theory (Soft Skull Press, 2007), 2001-2016; Humiliation (Picador, 2011), 2005-2011; The Anatomy of Harpo Marx (University of California Press, 2012), 1996-2017; Blue Stranger With Mosaic Background (Turtle Point Press, 2012), 2001-2011; My 1980s and Other Essays (Farrar, Straus, and Giroux, 2013), 1999–2013; The Pink Trance Notebooks (Nightboat Books, 2015), 2012-2015; Notes on Glaze: 18 Photographic Investigations (Cabinet Books, 2016), 2010–2015; Camp Marmalade (Nightboat Books, 2018), 2014-2018; Unpublished writings and early publications, 1968-2016; Other writings, 1960-2017.

A Beinecke Library archivist identified the boxes within this series as primarily containing writings based on the description written by Wayne Koestenbaum. The sequence of the boxes within each subseries roughly follows the order of the boxes as numbered by Wayne Koestenbaum. Beinecke staff preserved the order of the papers within each box as received from Koestenbaum, except where changes were necessary for perservation or collection management requirements.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Writings consist of manuscripts, typescripts, corrections, proofs, galleys, correspondence, research materials, notes, audiocassettes, computer media, and other papers documenting Wayne Koestenbaum's published writings, as well as drafts of unpublished writings.

did.unittitle: Ode to Anna Moffo and Other Poems (Persea, 1990)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: the revisions for this book, WK's first collection of poems, are all documented through handwritten annotations, changes, paste-ins, creating pages that are intricate, detailed fields and palimpsests, with an understructure of typed text, and several layers of handwritten comments and pasted variants. The first drafts of the poems (and often subsequent drafts, too) interleave drafted lines of the poem with diary-notes on the process of the poem unfolding.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: approx. 220-page typed diary (some of it written in "poetic lines," in the manner of the longer "Rhapsody"); its central subject is WK's experiences, that summer, playing the piano again after 12 years of not playing; WK had bought a Mason & Hamlin baby grand (used) in May 1992 and so had a piano in his home for the first time since childhood; the diary takes root in that 1992 summer experience of re-immersion in piano-playing

did.unittitle: Jackie Under My Skin: Interpreting An Icon (Farrar, Straus, and Giroux, 1995)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An earlier version of Jackie Under My Skin was entitled Virtual Jackie; it was composed between Summer 1993 and Spring 1994. The completely new version, Jackie Under My Skin, was written in Summer 1994. Some of the content of the two books overlaps, but they are vastly different in tone, structure, voice, detail, point of view, aesthetic intent.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: mostly handwritten (some typescript) notes in preparation for revision of a book originally titled Virtual Jackie but then completely rewritten in summer 1994 as Jackie Under My Skin; includes nine-page double-sided densely handwritten outline for new version of book, written in preparation for editorial meeting with Jonathan Galassi, the book's editor at FSG

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: research file of magazine photos and articles about Jackie, including an approx. 1964 intact issue of special-edition tribute magazine, Jacqueline Kennedy: Woman of Valor, and an original vintage approx. 11 x 8 inch color print photo of J. Onassis in fur coat on Martha's Vineyard beach with I. M. Pei and other architects, including J. Douglas Peix, who gave WK the photo

The interview notes are extensive; relatively little of this material appears in the final published book.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: xeroxes and ephemera connected to Warhol

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fifteen notebooks, of different makes and sizes, containing WK's notes for the Warhol project; largely, the notes were taken while WK watched Warhol's films, but other notes involve interviews and other research for the project. Sometimes these notebooks contain autobiographical/speculative diary entries, as well as phone numbers and addresses (Max Grand, Viva, etc.), ideas, notes for other projects, drafts of poems ("14 Oct. 1998"), phone messages, notes for WK's public lectures/readings. These "idea notebooks/research notebooks," though they sometimes overlap with personal diaries, are to be distinguished from WK's actual diaries (also in notebooks). An index/guide to these notebooks appears in the "Warhol: Notes" folder, also in this box.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A book of clippings and journal entries, beginning 21 November 2008. First page: "a year of humiliation." An intimate process diary. The clippings and entries are a conglomeration of documented cases of humiliation around the world, some horrifying, others humorous. Other entries describe incidents from the author's daily life on the street and in public settings. There is also the occasional scribbled quote from authors such as Freud or descriptions of film scenes.

did.unittitle: The Anatomy of Harpo Marx, draft 4

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many of the folders contain a process diary by the author about his compositional process and intentions for future revisions. In the above scope and content notes, the difference between 'significant' and 'light' annotations does not allude to the frequency of the author's revisions, but rather their variety; if the majority of the markings are underlines, checkmarks, etc., they are 'light.' If marginalia, deletions, and reordered text comprise the revisions, they are 'significant.'

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Clippings/articles, handwritten/typed notes, correspondence related to Harpo Marx project. Notes range from formal and academic ("possible list of 'thematic clusters'") to diaristic ("After therapy," "Ideas for revision," "just fuckin' dive in"). An excerpt of Anatomy of Harpo Marx entitled "Fragments from the Pillow Book of Harpo Marx (experiment)," which the author calls in the marginalia "too vertiginous and ungrounded." Quotes written on loose paper, by such authors as Dostoevsky and Nietzsche, appear in the final draft of Harpo. Different varieties of paper, from notebook to printer to back of bookmarks.

did.unittitle: Original Notebooks for The Pink Trance Notebooks

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 24 variously sized notebooks (Moleskin & other brands), containing longhand composition of entire unedited draft of The Pink Trance Notebooks. Author refers to these longhand notebooks as the actual "trance notebooks." Author also refers to these longhand notebooks as the continuation of his personal diary, which he stopped officially keeping, after he began keeping, instead, these "trance notbooks." Trance notebooks are numbered, usually on the first page, from #1 through #22B. The last of the notebooks, #22B, contains the ending of The PInk Trance Notebooks, and the beginning of Camp Marmalade. The notebook entries comprising Pink Trance are dated from 30 November 2012 through 31 December 2013.

did.unittitle: nineteen notebooks (original handwritten manuscript of the book that became Camp Marmalade )

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Set of 19 notebooks, of different sizes, colors, and makes, containing the original handwritten unedited draft of Camp Marmalade, which was at that point conceived of simply as the untitled sequel to The Pink Trance Notebooks. Each notebook is numbered on its inside front binding, from #2 to #20. There is no Notebook #1. The very beginning of the Camp Marmalade manuscript is contained in Notebook #22B [2013-4), which also contains the end of the first draft of The Pink Trance Notebooks. Notebook #22B is in Box 52.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thick folder with many different poems (and some diary entries), handwritten and typewritten, mostly from 1982, though some from later; some are part of a group WK called "Eavesdropping Poems," composed while sitting at cafés and diners in Cambridge and eavesdropping on conversations at adjacent tables; some of the eavesdropping poems were grafted onto opera libretti to form eavesdropping arias; titles include "Echoes of What I Might Have Been" (pantoum), "Writing Behavior," "Maria Callas Sonnet," "War Diary," "11-12," "A Mangy Girl, Estrella," "11-11," "The Night Before," "Violent Pleasantries," "Rhapsody at the Krimsky's," "Penniless, on the Streets," "Waiting in Food Lines," "Dramaturgy," "A Topical Paradise," "King of All Men," "She Bit off Dead Skin, More Than She Could Chew," "The Glass Pocketknife," "Miss Benton Works Few Hours," "Gritty Radiator as Raconteur," "He Bought a Century," "Etiology of Fire Hydrants in Suburbia," "I Wrote Her Number Down," "Advocacy," "Last Class Notes," "Miss Benton Works Few Hours," "Art," "Ketchup Poignance," "Addition,""Down Home," "Reubens," "Stacks of Notes Resolved," "The Bald Man Coming Back to Haunt Me," "You Can't Talk to a Pillow," "I Won't Clean Up," "Poor Sharon's Obsessive," "Your Seductive Narration," "I Started Jumping and Screaming," "The Strict Rules of the Dowry," "Made in U.S.A., His Fingertips," "Some Happy Tenants," "Specific Concerns in the Commonwealth," "Edgar/Chorus," "Scena: Lucia, Enrico / Il pallor funesto," "Lucia: Love Duet," "Spira sul mare e sulla terra" (Butterfly's entrance), "Io vorrei...pochi oggetti da donna" (Butterfly), "Addenda to Love Scene" (Lucia), "Queen of the Night's Aria," "Edgar, among his ancestors' tombs, bemoans Lucia's perfidy," "Lucia is forced to sign the marriage contract," "Raimando comes downstairs to announce that Lucia has gone mad...", "Learning that the stranger is his hereditary enemy, Edgardo, Enrico vents his rage...", "Love duet (Lucia/Edgardo)," "Optional verses in the love duet, rarely performed" (Lucia), "Sextet" (Lucia), "Regnavo nel silenzio" (Lucia)" [this poem was eventually published in Pavement], "Tosca, Addenda (in prison...)," "Cabaletta: Lucia cannot stay the ardor of her feelings," "Lucia's Mad Scene," "Madama Butterfly, fourth act, scene 1."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poem, including drafts; a later, revised, truncated version (4-29-84) also included

did.unittitle: Eavesdropped Opera Poems--Tosca, Madama Butterfly, Lucia

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many diary-like typed confessions and ruminations, as well as drafts of poems (a few on "While You Were Out" pink notepaper, and some poem/diary hybrids), including "Revenge Dream," "The swan is nothing miraculous," "if all the doors aren't closed," "Curls, shorn, on an empty table,""Into some rhythm, as into," "An extreme formalism," "and who was it on the cover," "He was from Italy," "is it too much to ask," "Uncle Werner and his red hat," "The moment she sees him," "Anna Moffo singing 'Caro Nome,'" "How to Become a Valkyrie," "A Poem for Roy," "Dream: Harvard Graduation," "Remembering the legs of young girls,""Anna Moffo," "The Red Porch," "A dream because you're not ready for more," "I lit the match," "I will make a cake," "The dreamt-of cup of tea," "Our courtship must end," "There isn't time," "My abode," "We heard the news," "There are always things to be done," "Quitting a job," "Can I believe that I have done with wheels," "Someone's Birthday," "The earlier wines meant more," "Dialogue between Black and White," "Home Town," "The Need to create a mosaic" (on same page as "Tired of her, in her black, in her glass cage"), "At last I found you," "The servants miss their toys," "Unable to predict the color of the skin."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: (On this folder, WK wrote a quote from John Cage: "there are no catastrophes"). Folder includes many handwritten and typed poems, including many "eavesdropping" poems; some of the poems are "Adequate Funding," "Languishing, Perhaps," "The Ambassador of Mexico," "Spillovers of Hostilities," "Overtures," a process note ("experimenting with content and aesthetics as important as researching for outdated eloquence"), "Massacre (Sonnet)," "I Can't Pay for the Guilty Half," "Well I Will Tell You This, Elizabeth" (a sequence of six poems), "I Never Talk to Them," "Bought and Sold," "Don't Waste Another Minute," "Keep Chalk in Your Jewelry Box," "Fast Becoming History," "Mein Kind" (handwritten on a napkin), "She is Petrified of Rubber Facsimiles," "Sonnet: First off, realize we're inside an arena," "Realityville," "Did You Speak to Tish?" "Arcane Technical Discussions," "When Music Kills," "Reams of Paper Clatter," "Compliments on the Meal," "Who Controls the DIrector?," "The Washed, Psychologic Street," "Not Designed to be Sung," "Sickly Child Who Rose to Acclaim," "A Very Nice Building, An Institution," "Two Minutes Ago, at the beginning of the summer," "Autobiographical Inventory," "Probability and Things Like That," "War Diary," "The Two Waiters Thoroughly Confused Me," "Self" (diary-like self-defense of teaching a poem that a friend of WK told him she felt was inappropriate for him to teach to 9th graders), "I thought Busoni was exaggerating it," "One Hour To Get One Telephone Call," "The Way His Fingers Curled When He Danced," more process notes/aesthetic statements, "Did You Find Anything at all that might link him to the plot?", ""The Smallest Record Player You Can Buy," "They are green ladies," "Blasphemy," "suffering isn't the only way," "The mule goddess speaks," "ultimately this is/childhood," "California," "strum your lyre, then," "Fears," "a sense of inferiority so deep," "Everything falls into the two holes," "I have new pictures on my walls," "Already Too Dark - Abortions," "Argument," "501 Summit Ave.," "Farming, Second Try," "Because I am afraid of being too neat," "Some days we sit in enclosures," "Corrugated Farmhouse," "Virtually impossible..,." "The Red-Skirted Woman / Family Island," "Avalanche," "The Color Wheel," "Or I am here at the wrong time," "Hospital Poem," "Dreaming Apart from Her Husband," "Return to an Elegy," "What's really worth discussing / seems shameful," "Fidget, distract, until what comes out," "Bicycling," "Sonnet: In Different Rooms," "Poem" (I will be urgent about the need for structure), "Poem" (I wish I had a dream / to put down), "For Diana, August 21, 1982," "The fact that my mother gave me through her genes/her birthmark."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Various poems, including typed versions of poems that appear in other folders as handwritten poems: "Spira sul mare e sulla tera," "Lucia's mad scene," "Lucia: cruda, funestia smania," "An Old and Interesting-Looking Piece of Paper," "Whom Music Kills," Lucia sextette, "Lucia: Dalle stanze ove Lucia," "Lucia: Tombe degli avi miei," "Lucia's mother, too, has a point of view," "Give This Guy a Dirty Glance," "I Won't Clean Up," "Consignment," "Bald Man Come Back to Haunt Me," "Lost Class Notes," "Etiology of Fire Hydrants in Suburbia," "I Wrote Her Number Down," "There Are No Catastrophes" (a sequence of fifteen poems), "I Started Jumping and Screaming," "The Strict Rules of the Dowry," "The Morality of Heteronomy," "Some Happy Tenants," "Prejudice, A Trait of Personality," "Specific Concerns in the Commonwealth," "Tu che a Dio spiegasti l'ali," "Io vorrei ... pochi oggetti da donna," "Queen of the NIght's Aria," "Made in U.S.A., His Fingertips," "Well I Will Tell You This, Elizabeth" (sequence of six poems), "Mein Kind," "Sonnet: First Off, Realize We're Inside an Arena," "I Hate This, I Hate This, A Pleasant Sport," "I Never Talk to Them," "Keep Chalk in Your Jewelry Box," "Don't Waste Another Minute," "You Young People," "Bridge Game Has Deterioriated," "Next Time You Might See an Office Building," "Some Kind of Dialogue" (sequence of five poems), "Can't Be Born with Silver in Your Mouth," "Sonnet: It's a Disease," "Sonnet: To Which He Said," "Massacre," "I Can't Pay for the Guilty Half," "Bought and Sold," "Fast Becoming History," "She is Petrified of Rubber Fascimiles," "Probability and Things Like That," "Compliments on the Meal," "Another Book on Sex?" "I Thought Busoni," "Men Mold Some Cities," "The Way His Fingers Curled When He Danced," "The Tie Does Not Appear in the Original (Chromatic Fantasy)," "One Hour to Get One Telephone Call," "Did you Find Anything at All That Might Link Him to the Plot?" "The Trouble Was It Lasted for Two Years," "At Sixteen, She Knew As Much," "Free Enterprise," "Man Cuts Off Hand to Please Goddess," "Jackie's Secret Life," "Maria Callas Is Dead."

did.unittitle: Miscellaneous Writing and Unfinished Poetry, Dec. 1982-April 1983

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: mostly typed, some handwritten poems prose sketches, diary entries, and process notes, including "My father's gone," "I'm being a little tight-assed," "Anything I've heard today," "Drama Piece," "Tonight nobody's around to fix / dinner," "Coloratura," "When we were children on our block," "Mrs. Perkins' piano," "The room is wide and very bare," "writing problem--writing block preventive medicine," "treasure chest," "A List of Them Men" (in nine different versions, each labeled differently--"random version," "half-random version," "kitchen table version," typewriter final version," etc.), "poem made out of leftovers (casserole)," "Nudism," "I Want To Go to the Combat Zone," "The Tendency of Men," "showers when the soap begins to wear away and soap" (sequence of prose poems), "Her French Name was Josette," "Richard," "the teacher claims the boy, last year, showed signs / of homosexuality," "a thank-you note for literature," "I used to appear shirtless," "Who gave me the flowers?" "It is difficult to say why the lovers' heads...", "I know two Peters," "Ricky's bent penis," diary, Jan. 4, 1983 ("what I feel after seeing Diana"), "Martha Culley, my secretary, enters...", "After sleeping with Kani on Thursday night, her gay roommate Lonnie drove me home...," "one event without any literary embroidery," "Mary Wallace, Anna Moffo, Marietta Martwick, Mrs. Johnson, Montgomery Theater," "Jeanine wore a white dress I had seen before," "I want to tell about something that really happened," "In the car with my mother," "I took the orange line to the Combat Zone to masturbate ...", "at first, writing to regain," "Edna Ferber"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typewritten and handwritten diary entries, poems, sketches, dreams, ruminations, notes, stories. On various kinds of paper (note cards, library cards, yellow lined pads). Pieces include: "character sketch" (9/28/78), "A Blue Rhapsody," "Dear Joshua," "Fables for Regeneration," "A Photograph of Auschwitz," "Childhood Letter," "Pompeii," "The Box," "the night after not getting into option three," "Lines in Rebellion, after a Loss," "impressions scrawled (typed) at two-thirty a.m.," "the difficulty of gender at a small age," "A Testament," "Waking up in Victoria's bed," "My grandfather"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Prose sketches and diary entries, including "I dreamt my mother had two faces"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poems and prose sketches and diary entries (typed on various kinds of paper), including "Wanita," "So I am too green," "This one's for you, Roz, for the books," "I don't need curves," "I am running down a dark road," "Nicknames for a Beloved"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: thick folder includes: Harvard Coop notebook (containing handwritten first drafts of the stories WK wrote the first semester of freshman year, as well as diary entries); many pages of densely handwritten and typed prose sketches, fictions, diary entries; poems (including "I dream of a baby," "Mother," "In a theater dark with our fantasies," ); a Harvard-Radcliffe Collegium Musicum program with WK's handwritten personal jottings on the back; sketch of "a story about a homosexual young man told in the first person"

did.unittitle: College Writing (1976-1980) and unpublished poems (1980-1985), including first poetry manuscript, 50 Sonnets (1983)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Many typewritten diary entries and autobiographical prose sketches (including a sequence of personal ruminations called "The Roy Papers")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thick folder of essays (final versions, with teachers' comments) written for college courses; some blue examination books, too. Subjects include: Confessions of Zeno & Cherry Orchard, Wallace Stevens, Waiting for Godot, Yeats's "Byzantium," Sir Gawain & The Green Knight, The Faerie Queen, Shakespeare, Mill on the Floss, D. H. Lawrence, Keats, P.B. Shelley, Frank O'Hara's "In Memory of My Feelings" (for sophomore tutorial), Renaissance painting, Egyptian sculpture, medieval frescoes, and "The Nature of the Primitive and the Modern Religious Experience"

did.unittitle: Harvard Exams and Papers, senior year, 1980

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Essays and exams for Harvard courses, WK's senior year. Subjects include: 20th century intellectual history, symbolist poetry, the book of Exodus,Colette, Rimbaud's Saison en enfer,Baudelaire's "La Beauté," Rimbaud's "Après le déluge," Krypton (for a science course), Brownowski's Science and Human Values

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Comments by professors on WK's Harvard senior thesis (its subject: Pound's Cathay and early prose and poetry): professors adjudicating were Andrew Delbanco (magna cum laude), William Alfred (cum laude), Walter Kaiser (magna cum laude plus)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Essays written for college courses. Subjects include Donne, Herbert, Walden, Paradise Lost, "The Significance of Public Opinion in Johnson's Decision to De-escalate the Vietnam War," Wordsworth's "Nutting," Emily Dickinson and Gerard Manley Hopkins

Folder includes obituary page, Dec. 2002, Harvard Magazine, of WK's college roommate Michael Brettell.

Also includes bookmarks with annotations, some printed-out emails, Bernadette Mayer's "list of journal ideas," and obituary (New York Times) of Alan Dugan.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thick folder with drafts of many unpublished poems, including"I've Promised Connie Francis" (the first line ended up becoming the title/theme of a poem published in Blue Stranger with Mosaic Background), "Sums (after reading Nicanor Parra)," "Ten Flies Congregating in the Bathroom, Some Dead," "At Pool Saw Hairy Stylish Guy," "Immediate Experience," "Just the Dirty Parts," "After Poems by Nicolás Pinkus (#2)," "Steve dancing with eleven girls," "peonies thickly outside my window," "Arteries," "Mutterings of a New Bear," "Orange T," "Alhambra," "attempt to discover a new line and new method," "Back Again," "Hotel Ritz Madrid," "April 11, 2005," "Insult," "The Stoker," "Atlas," "Carrion," "Two things I Didn't Tell You," "Requests," "Babies #1,""Once More at the Grave of the Ellipsian" (with affiliated drafts of acrostic poems for figures who died in 2004), "Bitch Fest," "What Fatty Wants," "President's Day," "Baby Food," "Sade Pedagogy Dream," "A few dream basics," "Vernal Equinox," "Her Death According to the Town Crier," "Spasmodics," "Indian Love Call," "Weird Poem Composed on Waking in the Magic Castle," "Jackie O dream," "Oh Darling," "They choose him for his splash," "He is sick so he got nominated," Cary Grant acrostic poem ("Capable of enormous diffidence, you..."), "Renata Tebaldi, Dead at 82," "Dream: I telephoned J.A.," "The Republicans Are Coming!," and "Poem After Visiting Juilliard Bookstore in Early December." Folder also includes obituaries of Carlo Maria Giulini, David Diamond, Renata Tebaldi, and Lisa Fittko; a 1977 NY Times article about Anna Moffo ("A Troubled Prima Donna Tries for a Comeback"); a list of "ideas for future works"; a page of notes for "a short book about Susan Sontag"; a clipping with the jotted note "Poem about Michael Jackson's reading matter"; a short page of preliminary thoughts for the book Humiliation; typed diary/dream notations.

Notes on several Fiennes films (Wuthering Heights, Schindler's List, Quiz Show, Lawrence of Arabia), and notes from news articles. Xeroxes of fact checking materials, expense reports. scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Preface to Jekyll Hyde by Joyce Carol Oates (signed); Anemone Me, a play by Bruce Hainley & Suzan-Lori Parks; Poetry manuscript by James Schuyler addressed to Penelope Laurans; Review of Elizabeth Hardwick's Sleepless Nights by Joan Didion, and Hardwick's Paris Review interview, both forwarded by Bruce Hainley; Bruce Hainley essay on Marilyn Minter in Artforum; Bruce Hainley frieze essay on Bruce LaBruce; Edward Field's "A Fling With Frank," forwarded by Field to WK; Xeroxes from Mary Butts's journal; "Feminism and Fascism: An Exchange" letters in NYRB between Adrienne Rich and Susan Sontag; an excerpt from Leiris's Fourbis (trans. Lydia Davis); Wet Dreams (early Bruce Hainley dissertation draft); That Various Field for James Schuyler; 1956 letter for Schuyler from Frank O'Hara; Two 1959 letters from Barbara Guest from O'Hara; Three letters (19567–59) from O'Hara to John Ashbery; Letter from O'Hara to Jasper Johns (1959); 1957 O'Hara letter to Kenneth and Janice Koch; 1951 letter from O'Hara to Jane Freilicher; 3 WK poems with appreciative note by Jacqueline Osherow; "Strange Attraction: Exaltation and Calculation in the Poetry of James Schuyler" by James McCourt

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of notebooks, legal notepads, appointment calendars, and scrapbooks created by Wayne Koestenbaum.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: A Beinecke Library archivist identified the boxes within this series as primarily containing notebooks, calendars, and scrapbooks based on the description written by Wayne Koestenbaum. The sequence of the boxes within this series roughly follows the order of the boxes as numbered by Wayne Koestenbaum. Beinecke staff preserved the order of the papers within each box as received from Koestenbaum, except where changes were necessary for perservation or collection management requirements.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: WK experimentally read his poems into the tape recorder as an attempt to figure out line breaks, and as an aid to revision. Cassette.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photo of the piazza taken by Steve Marchetti (on their trip to Italy in 1985) and a photo of Maria Callas, taped to the two sides of a piece of cardboard.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Research materials consist of printed material, photographs, a scrapbook, interviews, photocopies, audiocassettes, and computer media documenting Koestenbaum's research into Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, Maria Callas, and Andy Warhol.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: A Beinecke Library archivist identified the boxes within this series as primarily containing research materials based on the description written by Wayne Koestenbaum and grouped the boxes by research subject. Beinecke staff preserved the order of the papers within each box as received from Koestenbaum, except where changes were necessary for perservation or collection management requirements.

did.unittitle: newsmagazines featuring articles about Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: magazines include Life, Look, George Wayne's Rome, Elle, Vanity Fair, People, Newsweek, Time, Star, Paris Match

did.unittitle: two photos (reproductions) of Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, one in pixels
The Estate of jacqueline Kennedy Onassis auction catalogue

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Sothebys auction catalogue, in four separate brochures

did.unittitle: red binder containing 188 pages of handwritten notes in preparation for writing Jackie Under My Skin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: research notes, mostly from biographies and other historical books related directly or indirectly to JBKO; in addition to quotations and details, WK includes his own speculations, and seeds of thematic or conceptual commentary

did.unittitle: New York Times review of Callas in Tosca at the Metropolitan Opera, March 19, 1965 + Kennedy Library research ID card

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: folder containing Callas review, and WK's "researcher identification" card from his research trip to the JFK Library in Boston

did.unittitle: Large scrapbook containing hundreds of news articles about Maria Callas, from the 1950s and 1960s

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: WK was given this scrapbook, which a friend of his had found somewhere (in a curio shop?). The scrapbook pages themselves are falling apart, unfortunately, and most of the clippings (originally taped to the pages) have come loose. Not clear if the scrapbook contains any material about the Callas fan himself or herself. The scrapbook represents an unusually thorough and massive record of Callas's appearances in the media, especially during the years of her greatest fame, and during the moments of scandal. The clippings come from an international range of newspapers.

did.unittitle: Folder containing materials given to WK by Nathan Gluck, who was Andy Warhol's art assistant during the 1950s

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: WK, when writing his biography of Warhol, interviewed Nathan Gluck, who gave WK a variety of memorabilia. This folder contains: an envelope (Marymount Manhattan College of assorted clippings that NG collected); New York City Ballet programs from the 1940s (Balanchine); two original musical compositions by R. Sheldon ("Noël" and "Sonatina," dated 1947 and 1950), handwritten and inscribed to NG; postcards and letters; various ephemera; several 19th-century prints and plates (from books).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Restricted fragile material. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

did.unittitle: #1 - Andy's voice letter to Man Ray #2 Madden Marhola July 23 '99

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence consists of letters to and from Wayne Koestenbaum, including literary, family, personal, and professional correspondence.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Correspondence is arranged as received from Wayne Koestenbaum. Several boxes of correspondence are organized alphabetically by correspondent. Others are arranged chronologically.
Individual correspondents may be found many times throughout the series, in multiple alphabetical or chronological sequences.

A Beinecke Library archivist identified the boxes within this series as primarily containing correspondence based on the description written by Wayne Koestenbaum. The sequence of the boxes within this series roughly follows the order of the boxes as numbered by Wayne Koestenbaum. Beinecke staff preserved the order of the papers within each box as received from Koestenbaum, except where changes were necessary for perservation or collection management requirements.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: WK's record of his submissions of poetry to journals and magazines; each poem has its own note card, listing the rejections and (in the cases where the poem found a "home") the final acceptance; each journal also had its own notecard, listing the various poems WK had submitted, over the years, to that publication.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Professional papers consist of correspondence, printed material, teaching papers, reviews, ephemera, audiovisual materials, and other papers documenting Wayne Koestenbaum's career as a writer, artist, performer, and teacher.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: A Beinecke Library archivist identified the boxes within this series as primarily containing professional papers based on the description written by Wayne Koestenbaum. The sequence of the boxes within this series roughly follows the order of the boxes as numbered by Wayne Koestenbaum. Beinecke staff preserved the order of the papers within each box as received from Koestenbaum, except where changes were necessary for perservation or collection management requirements.

did.unittitle: Correspondence - (often encouraging) rejections and acceptances from journals

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters of rejection (mostly) and acceptance (some) from many journals and editors, often encouraging and detailed; and some literary correspondence (Alice Jardine, James Richardson, Ron Bush). Journals/editors include: Michael Cuddihy (Ironwood), Laurel Speer, Hudson Review, Boston Review, Iowa Review, C.S. Giscombe (Epoch), Poesis, Phillis Levin (Boulevard), Agni Review, Joseph Parisi (Poetry), Sydney Lea (NER/BLQ), Studies in English Literature, The Nation (Elizabeth Pochoda, Grace Schulman), John Keenan, Garrett Hongo (Missouri Review), Paris Review, Kenyon Review, Laurence Goldstein (MQR), Linda Gregerson (The Atlantic), New Letters, David Wagoner (Poetry Northwest), Georgia Review, English LIterature in Transition, Cimarron Review, Indiana Review, Brad Morrow (Conjunctions), Jerome Mazzaro, Poetry East, Alaska Quarterly Review, Martin Tucker (Confrontation), Mississippi Review, Pequod, Ann Snodgrass (Quarterly West), Chelsea, Donald Hall (Harvard), Yale University Press (Yale Younger Poets Prize), Credences, Mark Mirsky (Fiction), Hawaii Review, Robert Shapard (Quarterly West), Grolier Poetry Prize, The Pale Fire Review Rick Peabody (Sun & Moon).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Syllabus (readings and films), which includes texts by Richard Dyer, Gore Vidal, others, and films such as Mildred Pierce and Madame X. WK's handwritten notes on some of the films such as Niagara and Queen Christina, and a Xerox of Pamela Robertson's Guilty Pleasures: Feminist Camp from Mae West to Madonna.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Syllabus, which includes Nietzsche, Avital Ronell, James Baldwin, and others, handwritten notes on assignment ideas

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Syllabus includes Freud, Alice Notley, Sontag, and others, Xeroxes or copies of Michel Leiris's "Nights as Day, Days as Night," Chekhov's "Sleepy," Borges's "The Circular Ruins," an essay on sleep by Anne Carson, others. Correspondence about the course with Ruben Gallo.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Assigned readings include Hilton Als, Rebekah Rutkoff, Bruce Hainley, Maggie Nelson. Xeroxes of Jean Genet's "What Remains of a Rembrandt Torn into Little Squares All the Same Size and Shot Down the Toilet," James Schuyler's Selected Art Writings, David Antin's "The Theory and Practice of Postmodernism."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Readings: Benjamin, Stein, Breton, Genet, etc. A lot of WK's handwritten notes concerning assignments and in-class writing activities.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Readings: Wittgenstein, Frantz Fanon, Jamaica Kincaid, etc.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Readings: Fernando Pessoa, Francis Ponge, Anne Carson, etc. Xerox of "How It Feels to Be Colored Me" by Zora Neale Hurston, Marianne Moore's Complete Prose.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Authors: Paul and Jane Bowles, Laura Riding, Jean Rhys, Countee Cullen, Jean Toomer, Robert Walser, George Oppen, as well as composers Fauré, Anton Webern, Eric Satie, and Paul Bowles's music.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Student work samples, admin City College paperwork.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Central text: Poems for the Millennium, Volume Two. WK's handwritten assignment ideas (Ex: A poem in parts, separated by a typographical sign — a sign that becomes a subject of the poem, or at least one element of its content). At City College of New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Xeroxes or printouts of poems by W.H. Auden, Louise Glück, David Trinidad, John Keats, Gwendolyn Brooks, Arthur Rimbaud, James Schuyler, Marilyn Hacker, Walt Whitman, Amy Gerstler, Frank O'Hara, Shakespeare. At City College of New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Full course title: "Ear Training: Poetry, Music, Performance." Authors include John Keats, Jacques Attali, Dickinson, Whitman, etc. Composers and musicians: Scriabin, Persichetti, Paul Bowles, Maria Callas, Elisabeth Schwarzkopf, Billie Holiday, Sarah Vaughn, Liszt.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course research material including John Ashbery on Stein, and a printed excerpt from Stein's Making of Americans. Student list.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Robert Creeley, Allen Ginsberg, Anne Sexton, Amiri Baraka, James Schuyler, John Ashbery, A.R. Ammons, Alice Notley, Jorie Graham, Anne Carson, Susan Howe, Myung Mi Kim.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Syllabus comprised of Lynn Hunt, Jean Genet, Bataille, Lawrence, Dodie Bellamy, and more. Lots of student responses to the readings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course around Walter Benjamin's Arcades Project (no supplementary texts). Student responses.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Central text: The Vintage Book of Contemporary World Poetry. At City College of New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Poets: Wanda Coleman, Frank Bidart, Adrienne Rich, Eileen Myles, and others

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Central text: The Vintage Book of Contemporary World Poetry. At City College of New York.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course subtitle: Adventures in Postmodern Poetics. A study of a certain kind of poetry "where 'book' behaves as storage space, as box, as tunnel, as brain, as liquid, as crystal, as diagram, as briefcase, as dump, as archive, as soap, as weather report, and as failure."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Study of poets such as Elaine Equi, Barbara Guest, and other contemporary poetry, alert to "questions of sound, muteness, brevity, length, disclosure, stammering, dailiness, difficulty, and accident."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Perverse" poets under review: Dickinson, Mallarmé, Pound, Celan, O'Hara, Bachmann, Stein, Langston Hughes, José Lezama Lima, Marianne Moore

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Study of Pater, Ruskin, Rilke, Stein, Wittgenstein, Eileen Myles, Glenn Ligon, James Lord, Schuyler, Rosalind Krauss, Maggie Nelson, Sontag, TJ Clark

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Independent study offered by WK in which students would accompany him to art galleries around NYC

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course on Stein, Loy, Lorca, O'Hara, Ammons, Ashbery, Walcott, Glück, Myung Mi Kim, Lyn Hejinian. Syllabus, handwritten and typed notes on the individual poets.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course on "queer theory," gay/lesbian thought, experimental critical writing. Authors include Stein, Eileen Myles, Sedgwick, Butler, Genet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Later offering of queer theory course, with a focus on sadomasochism. Stein, Sade, Dennis Cooper, Deleuze, and others.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Later offering of course. Authors: Creeley, Jean Valentine, Ashbery, Schuyler, Mark Strand, Carson, Thylias Moss, Seidel, Bidart, April Bernard, Kim, Jacqueline Osherow, Amy Gerstler

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "A study of some modern prose fictions that propose 'reading' as one of the strangest adventures of which a human being is capable." Authors: Proust, Barthes, Deleuze/Guattari, Woolf, Ronald Firbank, Stein, Lydia Davis, Laura Riding, Morrison, Blanchot, Oates, Sapphire, Jabés. WK's handwritten notes on many of the authors. Secondary readings on Proust.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course catalogue with descriptions of WK's seminar on Eliot, Langston Hughes, Crane, Marianne Moore, Auden, Bishop; and WK's seminar on Wilde, Stein, Barthes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eliot, Langston Hughes, Crane, Marianne Moore, Auden, Bishop. Selected bibliographies on each poet.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course has a queer theory angle. Most of Wilde's nonfiction. Stein: Autobiography of Alice B Toklas, Tender Buttons, Four Saints in Three Acts, shorter selections. Barthes: Mythologies, A Lover's Discourse, The Responsibility of Forms, Empire of Signs, Camera Lucida, Roland Barthes. Xerox of Richard Howard's Roland Barthes obituary in The Nation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Nonfiction writing workshop. Student list. A collage of "Short Talks" from Anne Carson's Plainwater.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letters and journals of Madame de Sévigné, Byran, Keats, Dickinson, Dorothy Wordsworth, Charles Chestnut, Henry James. Essay by Langdon Hammer, on Elizabeth Bishop, included.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Course on Wordsworth, Coleridge, Byron, Shelley, Keats. Special attention devoted to gender/sexuality, "and therefore to thematics and rhetorics of appetite, pleasure, pain, disease, self-constitution, perversion, collaboration, incorporation, and decomposition." Students' annotated bibliographies included, as well as WK's notes on the poets.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Authors: Austen, Dorothy Wordsworth, Keats, Dickinson, Thoreau, Carlotte L. Forten, Woolf, Rhys, Lorraine Hansberry, Sexton. Lots of WK's handwritten notes. The seminar studied "the ambiguities of the personal letter, considered as a literary form, yet also as a genre isolated from literature we presently conceive it."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 11 typescript drafts, one galley, of essay originally written for film festival in Cologne, Germany. Plus two drafts/warm-ups for the essay entitled "Shine and Stupefaction."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 13 typescript drafts, with process notes. Pertinent magazine clippings. (This essay was WK's first piece of published art-writing.)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 11 typescript drafts of "Queering the Pitch: A Posy of Definitions and Impersonations." Letter of agreements with Routledge. Processing notes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Dirty Soapsuds": four typescript drafts. Invitation from Ruben Gallo (Guadalajara gallery).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eight typescript drafts. Plus Lange research material from Vanity Fair, NYT, others. A lot of handwritten preliminary notes for the essay.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Four typescript drafts of an Artforum essay on a Beecroft Guggenheim show, plus a galley. Lots of handwritten notes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Xeroxed/printed reading material: Sappho, Tsvetaeva, Pound, Carson, Cavafy, Hölderlin, Rilke, Moore, O'Hara, Ponge, Celan, Stein, Schubert, Kim, Glück, Schuyler. Two assignments by student Kathy O'Leary.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Catalogue with description of WK course The Pornographic Imagination. Course descriptions also for Contemporary American Poetry and Warhol. Correspondence with CUNY administration.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Handwritten draft of Recruitment Day talk.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: List of students supervised during studio visits. Invitation and subsequent thank you for participating in the visiting critic program at Yale School of Art.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Authors: Djuna Barnes, Beckett, Thomas Bernhard, Bolaño, Anne Carson, Duras, Marole MAso, Eileen Myles, Sarduy, Sontag, Stein, Robert Walser. Printout of Carson's Paris Review interview. Printed brief biography on Sarduy with remarks by Bruce Benderson. NYT review of Bolaño's By Night in Chile. BBC Bolaño obit. JM Coetzee and John Taylor essays on Walser. Assorted other research material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A coure—secretly subtitled "A Theory of Pleasure"—was the third offered by WK in a series "exploring intersections of literature, music, and performance." Authors: Rhys, Gissing, Wharton, James, Bachelard, Stein, Cather, Colette, Redonnet, Baldwin, Hardwick, Didion, Ashbery. Student work by Justin Yockel.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Six drafts of unpublished essay on Gertrude Stein. With notes on legal paper. Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library request form. NY Mag clipping on Nan Goldin. (This essay was delivered as lecture at the English Institute, Harvard U.; CUNY Graduate Center; and University of Michigan, Ann Arbor.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Seven typescript drafts and a galley of "Art's Hard Face," on Timothy Greenfield-Sanders. An outline and typed notes. Printed article on Greenfield-Sanders and Monica Lewinsky.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photo negatives and Cheim and Read catalogue list--material used in preparation for WK's essay on Pierson.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "The Uses of Doubt" Xerox

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Plus the Voice Literary Supplement review of Ode to Anna Moffo and Other Poems.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Five typescript drafts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Invoice from Timothy Everest Limited, plus photos of designer's work as donned by celebrities.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two drafts for proposal of fellowship to write book on Stein. Janet Malcolm's "Gertrude Stein's War" (New Yorker). To-do research list and handwritten notes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ideas: book on voyeurism, book on stars, book on Gertrude Stein, book on six performances (Judy at Carnegie Hall, Barbara [sic] Streisand in Central Park Vladamir Horowitz in Carnegie Hall…). Process diary, un-optimistic about completing Stein book.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Two typescript drafts

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Five typescript drafts. Retitled "Vienna in a Classical Mode."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three typescript drafts, galley, and a Xerox of final "The Pink Hotel," review of Didion's The Last Thing He Wanted for London Review of Books. Two article clippings concerning Didion from New York mag and Vogue.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Four typescript drafts of Foucault essay, with variant titles. Plus two news articles about a child molestation case in Guatemala.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Used in preparation for writing an essay about David Salle.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Clippings concerning cybersex. Note by WK on manilla folder: "I spent much of January watching Apartment 21."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Notes on meeting with Nan Goldin (typed)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eleven typed drafts of conversation; folder includes correspondence between Hainley, WK, and Artforum about changes and revisions.

scopecontent.p: two copies of a poster for WK's reading from his novel, Moira Orfei in Aigues-Mortes, at a Chelsea Barnes and Noble, 2004

scopecontent.p: a roll of large prints of color and black-and-white photos of WK, taken by Alexandra Penney in New York, in her SoHo studio

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: reproduction (oversize) of The Queen's Throat (Poseidon Press) cover, mounted on foam core (publicity artifact from a bookstore reading)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: three photographic prints by Claude Wampler, exhibited in WK's "Bathroom" show, Thomas Healy Gallery, NYC, 1998. For this series, Wampler stationed herself in public restroom stalls, left the door ajar, and then photographed the women who entered the stall, expecting it to be empty, and who were surprised to see Wampler occupying it.

scopecontent.p: Course catalogues for most of the years WK taught in Yale's English Department

scopecontent.p: WK's Johns Hopkins diploma for M.A.; stored in a mailing tube

scopecontent.p: Notice placed in New York Times daily paper by Vintage Books and Simon & Schuster to announce WK's Whiting Award

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Personal and family papers consist of memorabilia, childhood writings, school papers, and other papers documenting Wayne Koestenbaum's childhood; college papers; papers documenting Koestenbaum's family history; photographs; address books and a rolodex; artwork given to Koestebaum; opera programs; and other papers collected by Koestenbaum.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: A Beinecke Library archivist identified the boxes within this series as primarily containing personal and family papers based on the description written by Wayne Koestenbaum. The sequence of the boxes within this series roughly follows the order of the boxes as numbered by Wayne Koestenbaum. Beinecke staff preserved the order of the papers within each box as received from Koestenbaum, except where changes were necessary for perservation or collection management requirements.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 19 (microcassettes) and Box 116 (reels and audiocassettes): Restricted fragile. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned legal-size clasp folder-envelope containing WK's work from 2nd grade, including report cards, compositions, stories

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-size clasp folder-envelope containing WK's work from 4th grade, including compositions ("How I Would Change My Appearance"), report cards, notebooks; includes reports on "The Eye" and "Motion Pictures"; includes a notebook containing a dialogue between WK and his teacher, Bernice Nigh, in which he tells her a piece of gossip about Julie Andrews, and WK and Ms. Nigh discuss whether a boy should be called "pretty" or "handsome."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-sized string-tied folder-envelope containing WK's kindergarten work, including drawings, and report card.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned orange legal-sized clasp folder-envelope, containing WK's work from first grade, including drawings, and report card ("Seems to crave approval on his work; inclined to speak out instead of waiting to be called on.")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-sized clasp folder-envelope containing WK's work from 5th grade, including a picture book-report on Charlie Chaplins's My Autobiography, and a book report on The Queen's Awards ("The weight's goal is to kill the art expert.")

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-size clasp folder-envelope containing WK's work from 6th grade, including artwork (a torn-paper collage entitled The Affected), and stories ("The Magical Adventures of Pinkle").

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-size clasp-folder containing WK's work from seventh grade, including a report on Boris Pasternak, and issues of the school newsletter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-size clasp folder-envelope containing WK's work from 8th grade, including a handwritten draft of a short story (eventually titled "Tomorrow's Sun May Never Rise," it begins, "Noel fell over the boy's foot, and the class roared"; WK lost his handwritten final copy of the essay [he gave it to a classmate who never returned it]); and an essay, "The Day I Flew My Kite." Several issues of school newsletter.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-size clasp folder-envelope containing WK's work from 9th grade. Includes a report on the Russian folktale (and a description of a short film WK made, "What Men Live By," based on a Tolstoy story), a report on Gandhi ("Gandhi: The Man"), and a long paper on ethnicity in California.

did.unittitle: Wayne - sophomore, junior, senior years (10th, 11th, 12th grades)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown legal-size clasp folder-envelope containing report cards and certificates from tenth, eleventh, and twelfth grades. Includes a term paper on "The Afro-American Experience in Literature," and a report on Hermann Hesse's Beneath the Wheel.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned brown letter-sized clasp folder envelope containing WK's work from 3rd grade. Includes report cards, compositions, drawings. Teacher writes of his work habits: "Good but at times talks constantly."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 50-page term paper, handwritten, on the Wild West (fourth grade)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 62-page term paper, handwritten, on Venezuela (fifth grade)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 58-page term paper, handwritten, with enclosures, on Massachusetts (fourth or fifth grade)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 93-page term paper, handwritten, on the history of motion pictures (sixth grade). Contains complete (handwritten) lists of the films of Charlie Chaplin, Greta Garbo, Judy Garland, Clark Gable, Loretta Young, Joan Crawford, Kim Novak, and Grace Kelly.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three drawings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oldfashioned clasp folder-envelope containing several handwritten letters from WK to his parents; programs from WK's concerts at Harvard; WK's high-school valedictory speech (with some handwritten annotations/corrections by his mother); a 1977 short story by WK ("Grace"); a letter of recommendation (for WK, 1976) from Marianina Olcott, who had taught WK in a freshman writing class, at Santa Clara University, taken during WK's senior year in high school; some Harvard report cards.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

did.unittitle: unpublished book manuscript by J.R. Orgel about the Jews in literature (in brown envelope, addressed from E. Orgel [WK's maternal grandmother] to Roslyn Targ Literary Agency, but apparently never mailed)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typed manuscript, written by J. R. Orgel, WK's maternal grandfather; given to WK by Ella Orgel, after J.R. Orgel's death. Chapters discuss the Jewishness (or, in some cases, anti-Semitism) of various writers, artists, and critics, including Lionel Trilling, Marcel Proust, T. S. Eliot, Karl Marx, Man Ray, Dorothy Parker, David Daiches, and many others. 151 pages.

did.unittitle: high school and junior high school "work"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Large manilla envelopes containing WK's junior high and high school work. Includes compositions in French; some poems; book reports; English compositions; notes; quizzes and exercises.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Red manilla folder containing a sequence of folk songs (in the style of German folk songs) written as a project in a world literature class took with teacher Bonnie Phelps [Salera] at Prospect High School. One of the songs, a lullaby, is dedicated "au bébé de Bonnie."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

did.unittitle: Nürnberger Lebfuchen box containing letters written to WK in childhood, 1958-1987

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: German cookie box (presumably from WK's great-aunt Alice Gutfeld), containing a host of letters to WK from family, classmates, friends, in childhood. This was the box in which WK, in childhood, stored these letters. Also contains address book from high school (junior or senior year).

did.unittitle: Music Stuff - programs, composition book, other materials

scopecontent.p: Miscellaneous musical memorabilia from childhood, including concert programs for WK's band, orchestra, and recitals; programs from concerts attended (Joshua Koestenbaum, Lynn McConahey, Witold Malcuzynski, Artur Rubinstein, and a signed program from Karl Ulrich Schnabel); mimeographed sources and information list on "mixed-media/environmental/improvisation and other related areas," compiled by Dwight Cannon, with whom WK took private trumpet lessons in elementary school, and who included improvisation in the lessons, and who brought WK (as participant) to a conference on new music/mixed media/improvisation; note from Aiko Onishi to WK's father; father's notes on a conversation (3-8-76) about pianos at Harvard; WK's Prepatory Summer School composition-book for his summer at San Francisco Conservatory of Music; letter of admission to Aspen Music School.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Signed photo, addressed to WK, of Henny Youngman, a relative by marriage. WK's drawing (on red construction paper) of the No, No, Nanette ad.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Mostly books owned by WK's great-aunt, Alice Gutfeld, in Berlin, Caracas, and San Francisco; two books owned in childhood by WK; Alice Gutfeld's menorah; wooden salt shaker with "Koestenbaum" name inscribed

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection of books (housed in box 126) was passed down to WK after Alice Gutfeld's death in 1977. She was the sister of WK's father's mother, Ilse Gutfeld, and emigrated to Caracas a few months after WK's father (Peter) and grandparents (Ilse, Ernst). All the books in this box were Alice's. They reflect the exigencies of her exile, and the resilience of her survival and flourishing. In many of the books, she has inscribed her name.

did.unittitle: Mobil Road map for touring Venezuela
Carmen Clemente Travieso, Las Esquinas de Caracas, 1956
ARTE - scrapbook composed by WK's father Peter Koestenbaum while a child in Caracas

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed, on the back page, "Pedro"; contains reproductions of art works, with PK's identifying labels.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Book dates (probably) from a return trip to Germany that Alice Gutfeld made in the 1960s with nephew Wolfgang (Manny) Munzer and his wife Luise, both of whom were Auschwitz survivors. Book contains a clipping from Time magazine, June 13, 1969, "Pain: Search for Understanding and Relief."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Children's book by WK's aunt Doris (Adelberg) Orgel, inscribed to "all the Koestenbaums." Taped into the book is WK's childhood imitation of the paper slot in a library book where the due-date slip would be tucked; at this age, he turned his own small collection of books into the simulacrum of a lending library.

Alice Gutfeld was the sister of Ernesto's late wife Ilse.

did.unittitle: letter from Peter Koestenbaum to Alice Gutfeld

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Three-page typed letter from Peter Koestenbaum to Alice Gutfeld; describes his financial plight, his worries about statelessness (citizenship), his deteriorating relationship with his father (Ernst), his conflicted feelings about a possible marriage (with a woman named Eris), his feelings about his father's second marriage (to Carlota).

did.unittitle: Alice Gutfeld's jewelry (in a silver Macys box, nested in a German cookie tin)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: three rings, two brooches, one watch; also, a tiny plastic troll (WK's, in childhood). One of these rings, the smallest a sapphire, is WK's; sapphire is his birthstone; the ring was a gift, in childhood. This Macys box is nested inside a larger silver tin cookie box, in which Alice Gutfeld's letters, photos, and other collectibles were stored. WK received this box from his father, after Alice died; all of the Alice Gutfeld memorabilia housed in Box 128 were originally stored within the cookie tin.

These clippings are taped onto scrapbook pages; the entire scrapbook, kept by an unidentified fan, is housed in Box 85, in WK's archive; the pages in the Box 130 folder were taken from the scrapbook and placed in protective archival sleeves.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: WK's stamp collection, approximately 1968-1972. Extensive. Also includes, in a manilla envelope, tucked inside the blue binder, a smaller set of stamps, affixed to white paper; this folder was a gift from Mr. Baer, an older gentleman from the musical community--a friend or patron of the first cello teacher (Cheryll Melott) of Wayne's brother Joshua--who got word of WK's interest in stamps, and gave him this collection, as well as, perhaps, others of the stamps that WK included in his own "deluxe album." Mr. Baer appears in The Pink Trance Notebooks (2015): "how did the news travel / to Mr. Baer that I / collected stamps and wanted / supplements to my horde?"

did.unittitle: small white box, containing glassine envelopes with stamps, waiting to be affixed in WK's "Statesman Deluxe Album."

scopecontent.p: Either WK lost interest in the collection, or there wasn't room in his album for these particular stamps, or they were duplicates of stamps already in his album.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Printed materials consist of serials and other publications containing contributions by Wayne Koestenbaum, interviews with him, and reviews of his work, as well as books by Koestenbaum.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: A Beinecke Library archivist identified the boxes within this series as primarily containing printed materials based on the description written by Wayne Koestenbaum. The sequence of the boxes within this series roughly follows the order of the boxes as numbered by Wayne Koestenbaum. Beinecke staff preserved the order of the papers within each box as received from Koestenbaum, except where changes were necessary for perservation or collection management requirements.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Star Blurbs," in Commie Pinko Guy. ed. Bruce Hainley. London: Raven Row and Koenig Books, 2015. pp. 3-18.
"Play-Doh Fun Factory Aesthetics," in Salmagundi Magazine, 50th Anniversary Series Vol. 3: The Nonfiction Issue. Saratoga Springs, NY: Skidmore College, Fall 2015-Winter 2016. pp. 669-675.
- Also appears in Salmagundi: 45th Anniversary Issue, No. 168-169 (Fall 2010): 41-47
"Five Fassbinder Scenes," Salamgundi Magazine, 40th Anniversary Issue. Fall 2005- Winter 2006. pp. 58-70.
Untitled ("This experience might not be intense enough…"), in Merry Art. Hannah Barry & Donatien Grau, eds. London: Hannah Barry Gallery, 2015. pp. 79-80.
"My Brief Apprenticeship with John Barth," in John Barth: A Body of Words. Victoria, TX: Dalkey Archive Press, 2016. pp. 93-99.
"#2 [elegant toplessness stoned in stairwell]," in Hotel #2. London: Intypelibra. Spring, 2017. pp. 5-16.
"Fan-Mail," in conversation with Peter Rehberg & Brigitte Weingart, in zfm. Zürich/Berlin: diaphanes. 2017. pp. 89-103.
"The Secret Melancholy of Adam McEwan," in I Think I'm in Love: Adam McEwan. Aspen Art Museum, 2017. pp. 125-170.

"Tipping Over into Reverie: Jimmy DeSana," in Pioneer Works Journal (Intercourse #5) 2017. Brooklyn: Pioneer Works Press, 2017. pp. 185-195.
"Regarding a Little Poem by Max Jacob," in The Animated Reader: Poetry of "Surround Audience." Brian Droitcour, ed. New York: New Museum, 2015. p. 191
"In Defense of Nuance," foreword to A Lover's Discourse: Fragments by Roland Barthes. Richard Howard, trans. New York: Hill and Wang, 2010. pp. ix-xx.
"Trance Notebook #1 [I believe in ruin]," in Animal Shelter. South Pasadena, CA: Semiotext(e), 2015. pp. 80-87.
"Maybe you were too hot this afternoon.: Fables for La Pointe Courte," in Fireflies: words and art inspired by film. #5. Berlin: 2017. pp. 18-23.
"Watching Wayne," interview by Lawrence Ypil, in We Might as Well Call it the Critical Essay: Seneca Review. John D'agata, ed. Geneva, NY: Hobart and William Smith Colleges Press. pp. 47-54.

"My 1980s," and "Of Jackie Onassis and the Stargazer in Every Heart," in Life is Short – Art is Shorter: In Praise of Brevity. David Shields and Elizabeth Cooperman, eds. Portland, OR: Hawthorne Books & Literary Arts, 2014. pp. 137-148; 221-224.

"Wayne Koestenbaum and Maggie Nelson in Conversation," in What is Poetry? (Just Kidding, I Know You Know): Interviews from the Poetry Project Newsletter (1983-2009). Seattle: Wave Books, 2017. pp. 344-352.
"Wayne Koestenbaum on [Paul Klee's] Untitled," in San Francisco Museum of Art 360: Views on the Collection. Judy Bloch and Suzanne Stein, eds. San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, 2016. p. 196.
"The Blob," in Glenn Ligon: Encounters and Collisions. Glenn Ligon, Alex Farquharson, and Francesco Manacorda, eds. London: Nottingham Contemporary and Tate, 2015. pp. 202- 210.

Untitled ("My poetis—anal, alimentary, abstract—resembles…"), in Afflict the Comfortable. Ian Berry and Robert Boyers, eds. Saratoga Springs, NY: The Francis Young Tang Teaching Museum and Art Gallery, Skidmore College, 2015. p. 77.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents

Koestenbaum, Wayne. Hotel Theory. Brooklyn, New York: Soft Skull Press, 2007. (paperback original)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Koestenbaum, Wayne. The Anatomy of Harpo Marx. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 2012. (hardcover)
Koestenbaum, Wayne. The Anatomy of Harpo Marx. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 2012. (paperback)
Koestenbaum, Wayne. The Anatomy of Harpo Marx. Berkeley and Los Angeles: University of California Press, 2012. (bound galleys—uncorrected page proof)
Koestenbaum, Wayne. Andy Warhol. New York: Open Road Integrated Media, 2015. (Reissue, in conjunction with ebook. Without illustrations, which had been included in all other print editions.)
Koestenbaum, Wayne. My 1980s & Other Essays. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2013. (paperback original)
Koestenbaum, Wayne. My 1980s & Other Essays. New York: Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2013. (preview edition / bound galleys)

Koestenbaum, Wayne. The Pink Trance Notebooks. New York: Nightboat Books, 2015. (advance uncorrected proof)
Koestenbaum, Wayne. Notes on Glaze: 18 Photographic Investigations. New York: Cabinet Books, 2016.
Koestenbaum, Wayne. #1 [ I despise always trying to be an intellectual ]. Lexington, Kentucky: The King Library Press, University of Kentucky Libraries, 2015. Limited edition (50 copies) chapbook.
Koestenbaum, Wayne. The Rape of Rusty. Portland, Oregon: The Back Room, 2006. Limited- edited chapbook, in a series curated and edited by Matthew Stadler.

The Photography of Robert Giard: Portraits, Landscapes, Still Lifes, and Figurative Work. New Haven, CT: Jonathan Edwards College, Yale University, 2006. Chapbook including Giard's photo of WK, p. 76.
"The Rape of Rusty," in The Back Room: An Anthology, ed. Matthew Stadler. Portland, Oregon: Clear Cut Press, n.d. pp. 104-125.

"Five Poems: Fauré, Prelude, opus 103, number 3 ("Alone on a promontory"); Scriabin, Prelude, opus 11, number 9 ("I'm a kid with problems"); Chopin, Mazurka, opus 33, number 1 ("I tied my tubes tonight"); Scriabin, Prelude, opus 22, number 2 ("Tautology"); Hotel Theory Reader. Ed. Sohrab Mohebbi in collaboration with Ruth Estévez. Los Angeles: Fillip Editions, REDCAT, 2016. Pages 165- 174. This book is an anthology of texts exploring the exhibition Hotel Theory, named after WK's Hotel Theory. WK gave a piano/vocal performance in conjunction this exhibition: these poems are transcriptions of his improvised Sprechstimme soliloquies.
"What Is the Porn Punctum?" in Spike Art Quarterly Kunstmagazin #53, Autumn 2017, Deutsche/English. pp. 61-63.
"On Smells," in After Montaigne: Contemporary Essayists Cover the Essays. Ed. David Lazar and Patrick Madden. Athens, Georgia: University of Georgia Press, 2015, pp. 37-41.
Visionary Painting, curated by Alex Katz. Catalogue for exhibition at Colby College Museum of Art, Waterville, Maine, June 1-August 27, 2017. Includes a painting by WK: Jeff with Torqued Wrist.
Dream Closet, ed. Matthew Burgess. New York: Secretary Press, 2015. Includes painting by WK: Nightwood. Page 41.
Nikolopoulos, Angelo. Obscenely Yours. Farmington, Maine: Alice James Books, 2013. Cover illustration is a painting by WK: Angel with Blue Wings.
"Trance Notebook #2 [nerdy questions about exact pitch]," in Mirror-Touch Synaesthesia: Thresholds of Empathy with Art, ed. Daria Martin. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2018. pp. 251-260.

True Believers. Catalog for three-person exhibition at Brian Morris Gallery, New York, NY, January 22-February 21, 2015. Includes essay by Geoffrey Young about WK's paintings. Reproductions of four WK paintings.

Jackie O libretto, bilingual edition (Italian/English), published in conjunction with performance of Daugherty/Koestenbaum opera Jackie O at Teatro Rossini, Lugo Opera Festival, Italy, 3-5 April 2008. Program for opera tucked into book.
"Maggie Nelson and Wayne Koestenbaum in conversation," in The Poetry Project Newsletter #207 (April/May 2006): 20-23.

"Color Me Glenn," in Coloring: New Work by Glenn Ligon. Published in conjunction with the exhibition Coloring: New Work by Glenn Ligon, Oct. 22, 2000-March 11, 2001, at The Walker Art Center, Minneapolis, MN. Pages 9-12.
OUT magazine, December 2011/January 2012: WK included in "the one hundred most compelling people of 2011," with a photo. Page 105.
"Sebald Sandwich," in This Land: New Media from the Middle of America, Vol. 3, Issue 4, February 15, 2012. Page 11.
Interview with WK by Kat Stoeffel (entitled "Shame and Fortune: CUNY Poet Wayne Koestenbaum is simply mortified"), in New York Observer, August 29-September 5, 2011, by Kat Stoeffel, p. A15.
Review of WK's Humiliation, in OUT magazine, August 2011, p. 37. Includes photo of WK by Iké Udé.
"Culture vulture," profile of WK by David Noh (in conjunction with Cleavage), in New York Blade News, January 19, 2001, p. 19.
